name: X10 (home automation protocol)
short_description: The first general-purpose home automation protocol, using household electrical wiring to switch lights and appliances by room from a central controller.
long_description: Developed in 1975 by Pico Electronics of Glenrothes, Scotland, X10 encodes digital commands as brief 120 kHz bursts riding the zero crossings of the AC power waveform — meaning any outlet in the house can carry control signals without new wiring. A small command console sends house-and-unit codes that lamp modules and appliance modules decode to switch on, off, or dim. Products reached RadioShack and Sears shelves in 1978, making X10 the first mass-market home automation system. It matters for the Physical AI lineage because it established the architectural pattern — a central controller distributing commands to embedded modules throughout a domestic environment — that later smart-home platforms (CEBus, Z-Wave, Zigbee, Matter) all inherit.
ai_note: X10 is a pure powerline signaling protocol; commands are pre-programmed on/off/dim codes with no machine learning, inference, or adaptive behavior of any kind.

images.caption: An X10 smart home adapter module, which plugs into a wall outlet to receive powerline commands and switch a connected appliance on or off.
images.alt_text: A white plastic X10 home automation adapter module plugged into a standard electrical outlet, with a second outlet socket on its face and a small indicator light.

sources.title: X10 (industry standard)
sources.access_notes: Covers founding by Pico Electronics in 1975, powerline signaling technique, retail availability from 1978, module types, limitations, and successor protocols.

sources.title: The X10 Home Automation Protocol — Specs and History
sources.access_notes: Explains that the name X10 derives from it being Pico Electronics' tenth project, and traces the Accutrac 4000 predecessor that inspired powerline control.
